title: Thailand Needs to Soak Its Own Rich, Not Just Wealthy Tourists
gist: Thailand is betting that fewer, wealthier tourists can replace lost volume, but the author argues this 'soak the rich tourist' strategy is a loser given tourism-dependent infrastructure built for volume; the real fix is taxing Thailand's own wealthy.

insights.claim: Thailand received just 33 million foreign arrivals in 2026, far below the record 40 million it recorded in 2019, and is betting fewer wealthy tourists can generate the same revenue.
insights.speaker: Mark S. Cogan
insights.so_what: Volume shortfall of ~7 million arrivals is a real revenue hole the luxury pivot must offset.
insights.locator: After receiving just 33 million foreign arrivals

insights.claim: Thailand's tourism decline is driven by a strong baht, a rising Vietnam, and a transnational crime problem hampering interest.
insights.speaker: Mark S. Cogan
insights.so_what: Structural competitive and currency headwinds, not just cyclical demand, are eroding Thailand's tourism moat.
insights.locator: Thailand is suffering not only from a decline

insights.claim: The author argues Thailand's luxury-tourism solution is a loser because its infrastructure is built for high volume, and it should instead tax the wealthy people at home.
insights.speaker: Mark S. Cogan
insights.so_what: Points to domestic wealth taxation as the fiscally sounder lever than chasing high-end tourists.
insights.quote: there are other wealthy people to “soak” right at home
insights.locator: Thailand is suffering not only from a decline

insights.claim: Twenty percent of the Thai economy depends on tourism, much of it low-margin operators like tuk-tuk drivers, tour operators, small hotels, and local restaurants that a luxury pivot would bypass.
insights.speaker: Mark S. Cogan
insights.so_what: An exclusive-tourism model risks hollowing out the mass-market SME base that anchors 20% of GDP.
insights.locator: Twenty percent of the Thai economy depends on tourism
